skill.name: csv-processor
skill.description: Parse, clean, transform, merge, and summarize CSV files using practical pandas, JavaScript, and command-line patterns.
skill.summary: Practical guidance for CSV parsing, cleaning, transformation, merging, profiling, and export workflows.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the high-severity crypto and backtick matches are false positives in Markdown and sample code. The skill is publishable with low risk because it teaches local file processing and shell utilities that users must run carefully.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives in Documentation
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution and weak crypto matches are Markdown fences, JavaScript template literals, and ordinary words. No malicious execution or crypto API was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows code fences, sample JavaScript, and pandas describe calls. I found no evidence of cryptographic routines or Ruby shell backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples that read CSV files and write output reports or split files. This is expected, but users should avoid untrusted paths and accidental overwrites.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples perform local file reads and writes with fixed demonstration names or caller-provided filenames. This is legitimate for CSV work but has normal filesystem safety risks.

low_findings.title: Shell Utility Examples for CSV Processing
low_findings.description: The skill documents csvkit and awk commands with output redirection. These are legitimate CSV tools, but users should inspect commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible examples for csvkit and awk with no hidden network activity. They can alter local files through redirection, so a low-risk warning is appropriate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read and Write Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that read CSV files and write output reports or split files. This is expected, but users should avoid untrusted paths and accidental overwrites.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples perform local file reads and writes with fixed demonstration names or caller-provided filenames. This is legitimate for CSV work but has normal filesystem safety risks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Utility Examples for CSV Processing
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents csvkit and awk commands with output redirection. These are legitimate CSV tools, but users should inspect commands before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible examples for csvkit and awk with no hidden network activity. They can alter local files through redirection, so a low-risk warning is appropriate.

content.value_statement: CSV work often fails because files have messy formats, missing data, or large sizes. This skill gives clear patterns for parsing, cleaning, transforming, profiling, and exporting CSV data.
actual_capabilities: Explains CSV parsing with pandas, Python csv, JavaScript csv-parser, and Papa Parse.
Shows filtering, column selection, sorting, grouping, merging, pivoting, and type conversion patterns.
Provides data cleaning examples for duplicates, missing values, whitespace, dates, and validation.
Demonstrates profiling, summary statistics, outlier detection, and report generation.
Includes chunked processing and command-line examples for large CSV files.
limitations: It is documentation and examples, not a packaged executable tool.
Examples may need library installation before use.
Large datasets still require memory and performance testing.
Commands and file writes should be reviewed before running on sensitive data.

use_cases.title: Clean Exported Business Data
use_cases.description: Normalize columns, remove duplicate rows, validate required fields, and export a cleaner CSV for reporting.

use_cases.title: Prepare CSV Inputs for Pipelines
use_cases.description: Merge files, enforce data types, split large inputs, and create repeatable preprocessing steps.

use_cases.title: Inspect Unknown CSV Files
use_cases.description: Detect delimiters, profile columns, summarize missing values, and identify data quality issues before analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Profile a CSV File
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner inspection of an unfamiliar dataset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use csv-processor to inspect this CSV. Identify delimiter, columns, data types, missing values, duplicates, and basic summary statistics.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Customer Records
prompt_templates.prompt: Use csv-processor to clean customer_records.csv. Trim text fields, normalize email case, remove duplicate emails, validate ages, and describe rejected rows.

prompt_templates.title: Merge Monthly Exports
prompt_templates.prompt: Use csv-processor to combine these monthly CSV exports. Align columns, add a source month column, validate required fields, and produce a summary.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Scalable CSV Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use csv-processor to design a chunked processing workflow for a 5 GB CSV. Include memory-safe reading, validation, aggregation, and output checks.

output_examples.input: A sales CSV with inconsistent date formats, duplicate order IDs, and missing regions.
output_examples.output: A concise quality report listing affected columns, duplicate counts, missing region percentage, and recommended cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: Three monthly customer export files with slightly different columns.
output_examples.output: A merge plan that standardizes column names, fills missing optional fields, preserves source file names, and validates required identifiers.

output_examples.input: A large event log CSV that does not fit comfortably in memory.
output_examples.output: A chunked processing approach with selected columns, explicit data types, row filtering, and final output validation.

best_practices: Profile the file before transforming it, including delimiter, encoding, schema, and missing values.
Test transformations on a small sample before processing the full dataset.
Keep original files unchanged and write cleaned outputs to clear new filenames.
anti_patterns: Do not overwrite source CSV files without a verified backup.
Do not assume every CSV uses commas, UTF-8, or a header row.
Do not run shell examples on sensitive files before reviewing paths and redirection.

faq.question: Does this skill execute CSV processing automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples that an AI assistant can adapt for your environment.

faq.question: Which languages and tools are covered?
faq.answer: It covers Python pandas, the Python csv module, JavaScript CSV libraries, csvkit, and awk examples.

faq.question: Can it handle large CSV files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes chunked reading, selected columns, explicit data types, and chunked writing patterns.

faq.question: Can it detect dirty data?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for missing values, duplicates, invalid emails, invalid ages, and outliers.

faq.question: Is it safe for private data?
faq.answer: The skill shows local processing patterns. Review commands and avoid sharing sensitive CSV content unnecessarily.

faq.question: Does it require extra dependencies?
faq.answer: Some examples require packages such as pandas, Papa Parse, csv-parser, csv-writer, lodash, or csvkit.
